title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] RACHEL ANDREW: So hello. AUDIENCE: Hello. RACHEL ANDREW: Oh,
that was very nice. So I'm Rachel Andrew. I'm the content lead for
Chrome Developer Relations. So if you've ever read
an article on web.dev or developer.chrome.com,
then you've benefited from the work of my
team of writers here at Chrome. I've also had a long
career as a web developer and as a writer and
speaker, teaching people about web development. And as Paul said,
right now really is an amazing time for the web. So while you're at
Google I/O you're going to learn about loads
of amazing new web platform features that are
landing in Chrome. There are some really big
problems being solved right now. The features that are
landing are solving problems that I've been battling with as
a developer for over 20 years. Some of them are things that
we thought couldn't be done. Now, I'm sure when you
see some of the demos and you hear about the features,
you'll be as excited as I am. But I also know that
for many of you, your first thought will
be about how long it's going to take before you
can start to use them. If something ships
in Chrome today, will it be years before it's
available across browsers? Now, perhaps you've
seen comments like this. I can't wait to use
this in 10 years time. Maybe you've made those
comments or at least had the thought in
the back of your mind. That was true once,
but the platform is changing far more rapidly
today than it ever has done. If we take a look at
the platform as a whole, the truth of the
situation is that features are going from one browser
to available in all browsers much more quickly
than ever before. This chart shows the median
number of days for CSS features and how that's
dropped over time. And we can take a
look at some features. So style container
queries, now, this was a feature that for years
browser vendors and spec writers thought couldn't be done. Once the code was
cracked, however, it became Baseline Newly
available in five months.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
So style container
queries, now, this was a feature that for years
browser vendors and spec writers thought couldn't be done. Once the code was
cracked, however, it became Baseline Newly
available in five months. So that's the time between it
landing in Chrome and Safari in September of 2022 and
Firefox in February 2023. And then this cascade
layers, which is now Baseline Widely available. This launched in Firefox
in February 2022. And in just over a
month, it was also available in Safari
and Chrome, making it Baseline Newly
available; just a month. Now, I'm sure most of us
can point to a feature that one browser or
another hasn't implemented. But in general,
features are becoming Baseline Newly available
very, very quickly. I could have picked from
a large number of features to make the same point, and
I'll share some more later in the talk. But we can just
take a quick look. These are some of
the features that were mentioned in the latest in
web UI talk at last year's I/O. So many of those features
were new to Chrome were mentioned at I/O last
year, and they're now Baseline. There's some outliers,
but a lot of things become Baseline in under a year. And this is the time to
Baseline for features that have become Baseline Newly
available this year, in 2025. This includes things from right
across the platform, not just CSS. Now, you can
probably spot there's an extreme outlier here with
content-editable plain text only. And I always find
this quite fun to see when you get a really old
Interop pain point becomes picked up, and it gets fixed. But again, we're seeing lots
of these features coming in at under the one year mark. I'm going to come back
to specific features later in this talk to find
out more about what's actually new on the web. But first, I want to talk a bit
more about the other problem that we have with a quickly
evolving web platform. And that's how can
we as developers take advantage without
getting overwhelmed? And how can we be sure when
the things on the platform are ready to use?
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
But first, I want to talk a bit
more about the other problem that we have with a quickly
evolving web platform. And that's how can
we as developers take advantage without
getting overwhelmed? And how can we be sure when
the things on the platform are ready to use? Now, if you've heard
any of my I/O talks in the last couple
of years, you'll know that the way we're solving
this problem is with Baseline. So as you've seen,
these big features, things that we've
wanted for years, are being designed and
implemented in one browser and then very quickly becoming
Baseline Newly available. And what that means,
how we describe Baseline Newly available, it's once they
ship in the core browser set. So that's Chrome
desktop and Android, Edge, Firefox desktop and
Android, Safari, macOS, and iOS. Now, the thing about a
feature being in Baseline is once it's there,
it's unlikely to change because we've got
multiple implementations in different browser engines. Any finer details will have
been discussed and agreed on by people from all of the
browsers in the working groups that cover these features. It's also the point, once
it's newly available, that anyone with an up to
date version of these browsers will have the feature
available to them. Then, after 30 months, they
moved to the Baseline Widely available status. Now, that's a status
where you should feel pretty happy to use
the features without needing to worry about nonsupporting
browsers, polyfills, and fallbacks. Working with the web
developer experience community group, which includes all
of the major browser vendors and developer
community members, we devised the Baseline
Widely available line to be a solid choice if you
haven't got other information. So you're building
a brand new site, and you don't have any
existing browser stats. You're going into a new market. Or perhaps you just want
to land on something that's got data behind
it as being a really good line a base things on. The thing is that
between Baseline Newly available and
Baseline Widely available,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
You're going into a new market. Or perhaps you just want
to land on something that's got data behind
it as being a really good line a base things on. The thing is that
between Baseline Newly available and
Baseline Widely available, there's actually
plenty of chance to adopt features earlier
because not all features are equal in terms of what
happens if the user doesn't have the feature. And not all sites
are equal in terms of the browsers used by the
majority of the visitors. And the really cool
thing about Baseline is that the data that we've
got that enables us to tell you what's Baseline Newly available
and Baseline Widely available is there for you to use too. You can use that to essentially
create your own Baseline for the projects
that you work on. You can use it to make decisions
about individual features that don't meet your chosen
Baseline but that are good candidates for
progressive enhancements or polyfills. So let's take a look
at how that works. Behind Baseline is
the web features data. This is an open data set. It's maintained by the
Web DX community group. And what it does is it breaks
the entire web platform down into a set of features and
by looking at the browser support data for
each feature decides the date at which it becomes
Baseline Newly available. This data is now complete. We've mapped the
whole web platform. And so as new browsers
release each month, it gets updated and
checked to make sure that within a week or so of a
new browser becoming stable, any new Baseline
features are flagged. And you can follow along as
this happens by using the Web Platform Dashboard. We announced this last
year at Google I/O. The default view
of the dashboard is ordered by the date at which
the features become Baseline Newly available. You can click any
feature, and you can see the dates that it lands
in the different browsers. To see combined data about
how the numbers of features is growing, we'll soon
have a new stats page, which will show the number
of Baseline features,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
can see the dates that it lands
in the different browsers. To see combined data about
how the numbers of features is growing, we'll soon
have a new stats page, which will show the number
of Baseline features, along with the features
that exist in each browser. Features missing in
one or more browsers are classed as
limited availability. They're not yet
part of Baseline. Now, this data is a
goldmine of information. And combined with
data about web users, and particularly with your RUM
data, the data about your users, can help you make decisions
about your own Baseline target. So last year at
I/O, we announced that the RUM Archive insights
had integrated with Baseline, and that was now available. It lets you take a look at
the number of users supported if you select a Baseline year. Now, this year the
integration has been updated with
the ability to filter for the top 15 countries, sorted
by traffic in their data set. So let's think about
Baseline years for a minute because so far I've talked
about Baseline Newly available, the date at which the last
browser in our core browser set supports a feature. I've talked about
Baseline Widely available, 30 months from the Baseline
Newly available point. Now, based on the information
you have about your users, you might think that widely
available is too conservative or perhaps not conservative
enough, in which case you could identify a Baseline
year as your target that provides just the right amount
of browser support for your site and audience. So if you look at
the RUM Archive data and choose Baseline 2022
as your Baseline target, that would include
all the features that became newly available
up to the end of 2022. The data tells you that just
over 98% of their tracked users would support all
of those features, and it also tells you the
features that are included. If you jump forward a year to
Baseline 2023, 93% of users have support for that
wider group of features. For more information
on the features that you get by moving your
Baseline target forward,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
and it also tells you the
features that are included. If you jump forward a year to
Baseline 2023, 93% of users have support for that
wider group of features. For more information
on the features that you get by moving your
Baseline target forward, you could head to the
Web Platform Dashboard and select each Baseline
year to browse the features. While you're there, you could
look at individual features. For example, if I was
interested in the small, large, and dynamic
viewport units, they're part of Baseline 2022. And on the Feature
page, I can see when they were implemented
in each browser. I can scroll down and
see adoption information for the feature in Chrome. Now, this data starts
in 2024 because that's when a use counter was
added for the feature. So you could see how many
people are adopting it. But you can see how over
the course of this year developers increased
their use of the feature. So having data
about general users across the world or
users in your country, that's really useful if you've
not got anything else to go on. But if you already have
data about your users, you can use that to
figure out the best Baseline target for you. So the feature
mapping data that I mentioned earlier powers
Baseline and the Web Platform Dashboard. It's available for you
and anyone else to use. And we're starting to see a
number of tools and products integrate it. Last year at I/O, we
announced that RUMvision would be implementing
Baseline into their product. This integration is now live. Because that's using
your RUM data, data about the users
of your own site, it can help you
identify the Baseline year to adopt based on that data
rather than a global average. It can also help you to see
if Baseline Widely available makes sense for you. In the data on the
screen, you can see that 99.7% of
users this site supported the widely
available features. You can learn more about
the RUMvision integration in this case study
on web.dev, where they describe the technical
implementation work
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
see that 99.7% of
users this site supported the widely
available features. You can learn more about
the RUMvision integration in this case study
on web.dev, where they describe the technical
implementation work they did to bring Baseline
to their customers. The exciting thing
about this integration is that it pairs real user
data from your own sites with the web features data
that identifies features in Baseline, right down
to a per feature level. So this slide here shows the
content visibility property with data from two
different websites. The first website is a
US-based technology site. Visitors have more
up-to-date browsers than those coming to the
second site, which is a realtor site in the Middle East. So therefore, in
addition to helping you identify your
Baseline target, RUMvision can identify
particular features that have high levels of
support from your users. For example, RUMvision has
seen five of their customers remove the lazysizes
lazy loading plugin and replace it with
browser-standard lazy loading because they realized through
their RUM data that over 98% of users had support. So even though lazy loading
of images and iframes is a newly available feature,
it won't become widely available until 2026, for many
people, it was a feature they could adopt much sooner. So I'll talk more
about using Baseline for progressive
enhancement a bit later. So moving on to other
tools, perhaps you're a Google Analytics user. We've released a tool that lets
you import your Google Analytics browser data and then identify
the Baseline target that's most appropriate for your user base. Again, this tool is built using
the web features data set. So you can export your
data from Google Analytics. You import it here
into the tool. It's processed on device. And then you get a
report like this. Now, this is showing
the results for web.dev. It shows slightly lower support
for Baseline 2022 features and slightly higher
for Baseline 2023 than the RUM Archive
worldwide data. But it's in around
the same region.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
Now, this is showing
the results for web.dev. It shows slightly lower support
for Baseline 2022 features and slightly higher
for Baseline 2023 than the RUM Archive
worldwide data. But it's in around
the same region. Another place you might
want to link up information with your own user
information is using the data collected by
your CMS or developer platform. The Web DX community
group has recently launched an
extension for Netlify that shows support for
different Baseline years and widely available
on your sites. And that will help
you decide what to target in your build tools. We're expecting to see more
integrations landing this year. We already have commitments
from Cloudflare, who are planning to launch tools
for deciding which Baseline is right for you and measuring
the impact of adopting new features in their
Observatory RUM product. We also have Contentsquare,
who will bring Baseline data into their tools to show
opportunities presented by different Baseline
versions and new web features. If there's not
already something that works with the tools you use,
if you've got an internal tool, or if you use something
that allows your plugins, the data is fully available
for your own integrations. You can use the API provided
by the Web Platform Dashboard or consume the data with
the available NPM package. We're also trying to make
this a bit easier for you. When RUM Archive built
the dashboard last year, they had to handle a lot of
mapping of user agent strings to browser versions
and web features. That was pretty tricky
and time consuming, but we've recently
made available the data and tooling to make that really
simple and straightforward. You can now map browser
versions to Baseline years and widely available using the
Baseline browser mapping module from the Web DX community group. That can be used as a server
side JavaScript environment. Or you can download
JSON or CSV files. And they're refreshed on a
daily basis from the repository. Interestingly, the
data includes mappings
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
from the Web DX community group. That can be used as a server
side JavaScript environment. Or you can download
JSON or CSV files. And they're refreshed on a
daily basis from the repository. Interestingly, the
data includes mappings not just for our core
Baseline browser set but also for
downstream browsers, so things like Samsung
internet, Opera, UC browser, and Android WebView. So you figured out a Baseline
target for your projects. Now, how do you make sure that
the features that you're using meet that target? Now you can obviously look
at features individually. We've been able to do
that for a long time, and there's a bunch
of ways and places to do that now with
Baseline data included. So perhaps you'd like to use
registered custom properties with that property. Now, @property gives you
a way to declare a type, syntax, and define inheritance
for a custom property or CSS variable. It makes using CSS
variables much more robust, and it gives you
additional functionality. You can use them to animate
gradients, for example. But is it available
for your users? There's an increasing number of
places showing Baseline status on features. So you might go to MDN,
and you'd find the Baseline information there. We've partnered with MDN since
the early days of Baseline, and now the majority of pages
show Baseline information at the top of the page. You can see how that's climbing
up as they're rolling out more of their pages. They've also got their more
granular feature information at the bottom of pages. So when we announced with
MDN the support for Baseline, they had about 20%. We were around 70% at
this point of features have Baseline support included. You can also find
the information by going to an
article on web.dev and looking at the
Baseline widget that we include in the article. And it's also included on the
majority of Can I Use pages. Again, you can see that climbing
as it's being rolled out across Can I Use, 333
feature pages now include the information.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
that we include in the article. And it's also included on the
majority of Can I Use pages. Again, you can see that climbing
as it's being rolled out across Can I Use, 333
feature pages now include the information. And again, you could find it
on the Web Platform Dashboard. And wherever you
look, you're going to find out that it became
Baseline Newly available last year and, therefore,
is Baseline 2024. So knowing that
it's Baseline 2024, you're probably going to find
it's too new, particularly if you've decided that
your Baseline target is going to be Baseline 2023. So OK. We can look things
up individually. And you might say,
well, we've always been able to do that sort of thing. And we're seeing Baseline
pop up everywhere. For example, the
Baseline widget is now being used in several
places on the web. CSS-Tricks built it
into a WordPress plugin to use on their site, which
is based on WordPress, and made it available
to everyone. But the next step is
to integrate Baseline into the developer tools
that you can use every day. Now, last year I
went to Google I/O, but I also went to all of
our I/O Connect events. And I went to Europe,
India, and China. And developers
all over the world asked me for
tooling integration. They said, yeah, this Baseline
thing, it sounds great, but we want it in our tools. We want it in the
things we use every day. We want Baseline to be
available while we code, not to have to go and
check every single thing. So imagine that you've
identified your Baseline year. You want to make sure that
you and everyone on your team has sight of it as you code. Here's how you get it
into your tool chain. So let's start with
code compilation tools, like Babel and PostCSS. What all of these
tools have in common is they use browsers list to
determine the list of browsers that they need to support. Together with the
Web DX community group and other members
of the community, the Chrome team help to
release a tool called browserslist-config-baseline. This helps you to configure
your browsers list
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
that they need to support. Together with the
Web DX community group and other members
of the community, the Chrome team help to
release a tool called browserslist-config-baseline. This helps you to configure
your browsers list targets in Baseline terms, like
widely available or Baseline years. To use
browserslist-config-baseline, install the package from NPM,
and use the extend syntax in your browser's list
or C file or wherever you're setting your
browser's list targets. With this, any tool that
takes a browsers list target can now be expressed
in terms of Baseline. For example, if you're using
Babel preset-env to add polyfills for
cutting-edge JavaScript, with
browserslist-config-baseline, you can be sure that
you're not shipping unnecessary polyfills that
have support for those Baseline features. Similarly, you might be
using a tool like PostCSS to downgrade CSS features
and to add vendor prefixes for unsupported browsers. Now you can configure it to
keep the modern CSS intact if it meets your Baseline targets. And then what about Linters? Now, you might want to ensure
that your source code doesn't include any cutting-edge
features at all, and that's the job of Linters. They'll warn you if they
detect any features that don't meet your Baseline targets. Using Baseline with
Linters is recently made possible with a few new
tools in the Linter space, starting with ESLint for CSS. ESLint for CSS has a
used Baseline rule. You can set this to your
preferred Baseline target. It'll warn you of any features
that are newer than your target. Of course, you can choose
how to resolve the warnings. You might replace that feature,
or you might suppress the Linter warning. That's a perfectly
valid solution if you're using a
cutting-edge feature safely because it's a
progressive enhancement. By default, ESLint won't
warn if newer features are used within
[? @support ?] blocks because unsupported
browsers are going to not evaluate those ones anyway. For your HTML Linting
needs, there's a community plugin
called HTML ESLint.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
By default, ESLint won't
warn if newer features are used within
[? @support ?] blocks because unsupported
browsers are going to not evaluate those ones anyway. For your HTML Linting
needs, there's a community plugin
called HTML ESLint. And it works just like the CSS
Linter but for HTML elements and attributes. So for example,
you might consider HTML features, like popover, to
be too new to use in production. And HTML ESLint will
flag that for you. Other Linters beyond ESLint are
adding Baseline support too. Stylelint officially supports
a plugin called Stylelint plugin/use-baseline. And it behaves just like
the ESLint rule for CSS, but it runs on
Stylelint instead. So Baseline support in
compilers and Linters, that's really great. But what about getting immediate
feedback about features while you're coding? Now, as you've already seen, you
can go and check each feature. But many Linters,
including ESLint, now have IDE
extensions that will annotate Linter warnings in your
editor as squiggly underlines. So with these new
Baseline Linter rules, you can see at a glance which
web features in your code don't meet your
Baseline targets. Now, while thinking
about IDEs, many of these have long supported
a way to hover over a feature in the editor, and you
see a list of supported browser versions. But it can be pretty hard
to understand if that means the feature is safe to use. You have to figure out if
there are any browsers missing from the list. And you also need to know how
new that browser version is. And browser version numbers are
getting pretty big these days. It's often quite hard to know
exactly how old that browser is. So to fix that problem,
we've partnered with the most popular
IDE used by millions of web developers, VSCode, to
integrate Baseline data directly into those hover cards. So you can hover over a feature. It'll tell you whether the
feature is considered Baseline and for how long, and whether
there's any major browsers that don't support it yet.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
of web developers, VSCode, to
integrate Baseline data directly into those hover cards. So you can hover over a feature. It'll tell you whether the
feature is considered Baseline and for how long, and whether
there's any major browsers that don't support it yet. Now, supported features include
CSS properties, HTML elements, and HTML attributes. Now, not everyone uses VSCode. What about other IDEs? Well, if you're using an
IDE that is based on VSCode, then it will inherit the
changes automatically. So things like Firebase Studio,
GitLab, Replit, and other VSCode IDEs will start rolling
out the improvements over the coming
weeks and months. And hover cards are also in
WebStorm, the popular JavaScript and TypeScript IDE
from JetBrains. All of these tools are
ready for you to use today, but we really are
just getting started. So in the coming months,
look for more announcements and updates about
other tools that are starting to
integrate Baseline, making it easier for
you to understand Baseline status as you code. We're also really
excited to see companies adopting Baseline as their way
to show browser support levels. So this includes web agencies,
site builders, and frameworks. All of these are places where
being clear about browser support to users and
customers is really important, and it can save
problems down the line. Last year, we saw UK web agency
Clearleft adopt Baseline Widely available as their default level
of support for client work, making it clear to clients
how they supported browsers. Site building platform
Wix have recently decided to use Baseline Widely
available as their threshold for adopting new web features. Their frontend build team said,
"Baseline gives us clarity around which features we
can support and removes the ambiguity in our tooling." Our developers now don't
need to spend time digging through the compact
tables in MDN to know if a feature
will work or not. They just look for the
green tick and go ahead. Baseline helps our
developers feel confident that their
tools will warn them
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
Our developers now don't
need to spend time digging through the compact
tables in MDN to know if a feature
will work or not. They just look for the
green tick and go ahead. Baseline helps our
developers feel confident that their
tools will warn them when they've used a feature
that we don't support. It also reduces the number of
errors and broken experiences we might release. And the Angular framework
team announced that from version 20, which
launches next week, they'll be using Baseline
Widely available at the time of release, as their
supported feature threshold for all versions. So you now have the ability
to have a better understanding of the browser support
your users have and also a way through
data and tooling to tune your development to meet
the needs of your audience. And while adopting
Baseline Widely available or a specific Baseline
year as your Baseline and feeling confident to
use all the features that fall within it, it's
worth remembering that not all of
those features are equal in terms of what
happens if a user doesn't have a feature. You don't have to
feel like Baseline is a go, no-go indicator. Instead, you could
look at it as a way to reduce the number
of features that you need to make a call
on whether they're OK to use as a progressive
enhancement or even with a polyfill. So perhaps you've decided that
Baseline 2023 is your Baseline target. Now, if you treated
that a hard line, a feature like Text
Wrap Balance wouldn't be usable as its Baseline 2024. It became newly available
about a year ago. Adding Text Wrap Balance to a
line of text, like a heading, tells the browser that
you'd like it to figure out a balanced presentation
of the text rather than doing something like
leaving a couple of words, one or two words, on a wrapped line. If you were
typesetting in print, you'd try and avoid
this situation. You don't like to have
unbalanced headings. So on this slide,
you can see there's two words on the second line
with a longer line above. And that's something you'd
probably prefer to avoid.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
you'd try and avoid
this situation. You don't like to have
unbalanced headings. So on this slide,
you can see there's two words on the second line
with a longer line above. And that's something you'd
probably prefer to avoid. If you add Text Wrap Balance,
the headline looks much nicer. It's a really nice effect. And the thing is,
there's no downside to using Text Wrap Balance. People who don't have the
feature in their browser get the unbalanced text
the web has always had. People with newer browsers
get a nice improvement. They get some nice
balanced headlines. And because of the way
that browsers deal with CSS they don't understand is
that they just ignore it, your unsupported browsers
are going to just ignore that instruction. Nothing terrible
is going to happen. So things that haven't
crossed your Baseline don't need to be unusable. You can treat them as
Consider Carefully. And in a situation where you
decided to use a polyfill, you can then just
use your RUM data to see when it's time to
remove that polyfill code, just like the people who
learned that they could remove the lazysizes
plugin that I talked about earlier in the talk. So I started this
talk by telling you how the web was improving
faster than ever before. And you've now seen how you
can use Baseline to help you to take advantage of it. I want to come
back to that point and to talk about
some features that are Baseline Newly available,
or that we expect soon will be. So last year at I/O, Una
presented new CSS and UI features. Many of them at that
time were only in Chrome, or they missed implementation
in one browser. Now a lot of those are
now newly available. I already talked
about app property. That was one of the
features covered this year. That was a Chrome first feature. It's now newly available. The CSS light-dark function
first landed in Firefox and then came to Chrome. In May last year,
Safari shipped it. It became Baseline
Newly available. This function lets
you write styles for color themes in a
much more concise way.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U
text:
The CSS light-dark function
first landed in Firefox and then came to Chrome. In May last year,
Safari shipped it. It became Baseline
Newly available. This function lets
you write styles for color themes in a
much more concise way. Instead of creating
a block of color for your main color theme
and a user preference query, you can use the
light-dark functions to describe these values
in a single line of code. Also helpful for

Contentful Paint, LCP, and Interaction to Next
Paint, INP metrics, by implementing the

title: Google I/O 2025 – beYbnNT_02U

and Interaction to Next
Paint, INP metrics, by implementing the
Largest Contentful Paint API and the Event Timing
API across browsers. LCP reports the render time of
the largest heading and image visible in the viewport
relative to when the user navigates to the page. And a good LCP value
is 2.5 seconds or less. INP is a metric that assesses
responsiveness using data from the Event Timing API. INP observes the latency
of all interactions a user has made with the page
and reports a single value, which all or nearly all of
those interactions are beneath. If you've got a low INP, that
means the page was consistently able to respond quickly to all
or the vast majority of user interactions. So with these two
core Web Vitals being part of interop
2025, you should be able to measure them
across all browsers once they become

element is collapsed. Outside of the summary is the
content of the details element. Now, that's hidden until someone

nice improvements,
